Co-designing training for care-home staff to improve patient relationships by understanding touch interactions
The UK’s aging population includes many older people with dementia who need to be compassionately and competently cared for
in both care/nursing homes and at home. Meanwhile, the care-worker sector is plagued with recruitment and retention problems,
and the wellbeing of (often international) staff is a serious issue. When care workers learn how to use a caring and gentle touch
when interacting with people with dementia it significantly relieves distress, anxiety, and agitation, and has important benefits for
the residents, staff and relatives.
Aim
This research will study how residents, relatives and staff feel about the way they touch and are touched during caring activities
and daily interactions in care homes. Based on what we learn, we will work with the residents and staff to design appropriate
touch training for care-workers and nurses.
Background

n a blueprint for a touch-focused package of training for care-home workers.
Objectives:
Objective 1: To observe, adopting an ethnographic approach, touch interactions between residents and staff that occur during
caring and daily activities in a care home.
Objective 2: To analyse the interpretations of physical contact and touch interactions in care activities that emerge from interviews
with 3 care homes’ residents, staff, and relatives.
Objective 3: To co-create an educational intervention focused on touch, tailored to care-home staff and residents’ needs by co-
participation design and practice development.
5. Project plan
The RfPB grant will support a research project involving care home residents, staff, and relatives. (The term ‘care home’ includes
nursing and residential homes.)
The project will proceed in three phases:
Stage 1. Scoping review - Map available evidence on the use of interpersonal physical contact (including the diverse types of
touch techniques) in clinical settings, and report on the types of evidence that address and inform practice in the field and the way
the research has been conducted. Using the Joanna Briggs Institute framework for scoping reviews enh

for people with dementia and cognitive impairment.33 The
ethnographic data analysis is an iterative process that starts during fieldwork and includes reflection, which is fundamental to
value the different interpretations emerging during the analysis itself.
Stage 3. Design of a training on “Awareness of Interpersonal physical contact during caring encounters.” Training design will be
guided by the scoping review and ethnographic study. Ethnographic methods ensure person-centred design and customized
interventions for effective implementation in specific contexts. An experience-based co-design approach will effectively and
meaningfully engage care-home staff in the organic development of the training, ensuring it is tailored to their specific
needs/setting and guided by their expert lived experience. Patient and Public Involvement and Engagement (PPIE) members and
stakeholders will provide feedback during the training development, and the final version will be presented during a half-day
seminar to stakeholders and staff. This will help to develop future grant applications for a feasibility study to roll out training on a
larger scale.
Study Participants

th and social care sector through stakeholders
and agencies, journal articles, conference presentations, public engagement events, social media, and digital platforms. This will
include local, national, and international impact in care homes and HEIs.
Advisory board
The Advisory Board will meet four times throughout the 2-year project. Members include NHS England (Jane Bunce), NHS Devon
Integrated Care Board (Stevie Morris), Chief Nurse for Social Care (Debra Sturdy), Skills for Care Project Manager (Lucy
Gillespie), Devon Training Hub-Primary Care (TBC), Dementia UK (TBC). These members, including health economist Prof
Hulme and neuroscientist Prof Cazzato (Liverpool University) will actively contribute to the study's development at all stages, from
design to implementation to knowledge exchange. The PPIE lead will facilitate communication between the PPIE and advisory
groups.

elationship with a care homes network, securing the participation of three care homes in South Devon.
South Devon is one of the most popular retirement choices in the UK and has a high density of care homes. A preliminary
presentation of the project to care-home managers in May 2024 resulted in fruitful conversation and practical suggestions to set
up the research, communicate effectively, and safely and respectfully recruit participants (residents, care-home staff, and
relatives).
The study will invite all residents to participate. During the ethnographic data collection, researchers will plan, inform, and seek
permission to observe caring activities and touch interactions. The consent to participate of people with cognitive impairment and
reduced ability will be facilitated by relatives or trusted carers, who will be supported to create the right environment to conduct
unstructured interviews. Residents, families, and staff do not have to participate if they do not wish to do so.
The PI is an experienced nurse, and he will provide a safe and trustworthy environment for the study. Discussion has already
begun with care-home managers regarding shaping an ad hoc honorary contract fo

he safe and respectful
involvement of residents.
Equality, Diversity, and Inclusion
As PI I will use my knowledge and experience as departmental EDI lead to ensure that the principles of equality, diversity, and
inclusion are respected throughout the project.
Residents: As the research area is not very ethnically diverse, we will focus on differences in social background and class among
residents to enhance participant diversity, e.g. the ratio between privately/local authority-funded residents. The chosen care
homes include both rural and urban populations, and one is in a deprived area of South Devon.
Care home staff: The care home workforce, predominantly international staff, plays a crucial role in enriching the research
landscape. Their diverse perspectives, cultural backgrounds, and experiences regarding touch in care are invaluable contributions
to the research on cultural and ethnic diversity.
Research material, including posters, leaflets, and information sheets, will be carefully adapted and tested to meet the cultural
and cognitive needs of the residents and their relatives.
Dissemination
The project's key learnings and best practices will be shared with the broader heal

workers can lack confidence in dementia communication and the tools to manage difficult communication, and this
can lead to further frustration and intention to leave the job. Skills for Care highlighted the social care sector’s high turnover
(29%), and one factor is the lack of training or opportunities for development and new qualifications.31 Lack of meaning in
communication and person-centred care can result in staff compassion fatigue and burn out.5 Care-home nurses and carers are
frustrated by the stigma associated with social-care careers, and with being mostly an internationally recruited workforce with little
support and lot of pressure.6
2. Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to patients
and health care services?
The Alzheimer's Society emphasizes that research efforts should focus on improving the lives of people living with dementia
cure.4
today, not just finding a long-term Various governmental and local NHS initiatives are supporting the sector to deliver better
person-centred care,9,23,31 but workforce and funding crises are ongo

ing, exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic.17 There is a
real need for more effective care methods for people living with dementia, particularly ones that are easy for staff to learn. This
research is important in improving the health and wellbeing of both patients and healthcare services because it will:
a. Extend and deepen our understanding of the importance of touch in dementia care. While research
suggests that patients who receive caring touch experience less anxiety, physiological stress, and pain than
patients who do not,35 very few studies have explored specifically how awareness of interpersonal physical
touch in caring encounters can improve communication with older people living with dementia and alleviate their
feelings of loneliness and neglect in residential settings.13,26
b. Enrich evidence of how touch also influences the person initiating the touch. It is crucial to emphasise
how touch also influences and can benefit those who initiate and have an active role in the tactile interaction.
Touch is inevitable in care work, and documenting the positive impacts of touch and making it part of training
will make care work more meaningful and rewarding, and may reduce pers

touch and how they affect residents.
Methods
1.Scoping review
It consists in a literature review to map out What do (and don’t) we know about how touch is used in the care of older people,
dementia care, care homes and social care settings.
2.Ethnographic study in three care homes
An ethnographic approach means carefully observing people in their real-world activities, and using conversations and interviews
to gain in-depth insight into how people interact in care homes.
3.Design training on “Awareness of interpersonal physical contact during caring encounters.”
1&2 will give us the information to co-design training alongside care workers, customising it for care-homes and truly improving
care through more meaningful touch.
Patient and public involvement (PPIE)
Exeter Academy of Nursing PPIE chair Faye Doris and member Howard Almond (himself a dementia carer) support this research.
Our PPIE group of residents, relatives, and staff will ensure not only that study design and materials are appropriate, but also that
all ethical and safeguarding issues are covered, including consent. PPIE members will feed back during the

Research Plan
1. What is the problem being addressed?
This project responds to the current NIHR themed call on Dementia and the research priority ‘complex health and care needs in
older people’.
The UK has an aging population, and therefore a large number of older people with dementia who need to be compassionately
and competently cared for in both care/nursing homes and at home. At the same time, the care-worker sector is plagued with
recruitment and retention problems, and staff wellbeing is a serious issue.
Dementia and care homes. Nearly half a million people live in care homes in the UK6,23 (70% residential, 29% in nursing
homes). About 70 per cent of them have dementia or severe memory problems. By 2025, it’s estimated that over 1 million people
in the UK will have a diagnosis of dementia.8 Good care homes try to provide the meaningful engagement and activities that
improve health and wellbeing, but residents can struggle with a perceived lack of meaning and purpose – on top of the
dementia symptoms.3,9 Nurses and dementia experts observe how residents interact with their environment to understand which
situations.18,36
factors contribute to an individual’s anxiety or aggress

m developmental psychology to neuroscience and
philosophy.10 The tactile experience of touch, or a lack of it, affects the development of behaviour. Hertenstein et al. underlined
how numerous behaviours are influenced by a physical contact experience – whether or not the experience of interpersonal
contact is explicitly remembered.14 Touch remains an important research subject, with a recent Nobel Prize awarded for
discoveries related to receptors for temperature and touch.
Touch in Healthcare. Touch is an essential part of care activities, from providing fundamental personal hygiene and comfort to
performing clinical task/procedures. Touch affects our physiological and psychological well-being and an extensive body of
research has explored the beneficial and therapeutic effects of physical contact in healthcare settings.16 Nursing research
highlights how touch can influence patients’ wellbeing by reducing anxiety, facilitating communication and relationship, improving
a sense of presence, and infusing security and encouragement. While cultural background, gender, and contextual factors can
determine touching preferences, most studies also show that one’s history or experience of t

caregivers and older individuals with dementia in social care settings. Additionally, there is a lack of
documented experiences related to touch education in healthcare professions and few conceptual/operational care models that
consider intentional, mindful contact or a reflective approach to interpersonal touch.34
Therefore, this research seeks to fill such gaps by firstly exploring and understanding touch interactions that occur during care
encounters in care homes and to shed light on the level of staff awareness, different type of touches adopted, and residents’
responses. Secondly, it will lead to co-designing a pilot-training with a participatory approach by including care home residents,
relatives and staff insights.
4. What is the research question / aims and objectives?
How are the touch interactions during caring activities and daily interactions in care homes perceived by residents,
staff, and relatives?

To observe and understand touch interactions between residents and their care workers in caring and daily activities in care

skills,22,24
workers can lack confidence in dementia communication and the tools to manage difficult communication, and this
can lead to further frustration and intention to leave the job. Skills for Care highlighted the social care sector’s high turnover
(29%), and one factor is the lack of training or opportunities for development and new qualifications.31 Lack of meaning in
communication and person-centred care can result in staff compassion fatigue and burn out.5 Care-home nurses and carers are
frustrated by the stigma associated with social-care careers, and with being mostly an internationally recruited workforce with little
support and lot of pressure.6
2. Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to patients
and health care services?
The Alzheimer's Society emphasizes that research efforts should focus on improving the lives of people living with dementia
cure.4
today, not just finding a long-term Various governmental and local NHS initiatives are supporting the sector to deliver better
person-centred care,9,23,31 but workforce and funding crises are ongo

ing, exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic.17 There is a
real need for more effective care methods for people living with dementia, particularly ones that are easy for staff to learn. This
research is important in improving the health and wellbeing of both patients and healthcare services because it will:
a. Extend and deepen our understanding of the importance of touch in dementia care. While research
suggests that patients who receive caring touch experience less anxiety, physiological stress, and pain than
patients who do not,35 very few studies have explored specifically how awareness of interpersonal physical
touch in caring encounters can improve communication with older people living with dementia and alleviate their
feelings of loneliness and neglect in residential settings.13,26
b. Enrich evidence of how touch also influences the person initiating the touch. It is crucial to emphasise
how touch also influences and can benefit those who initiate and have an active role in the tactile interaction.
Touch is inevitable in care work, and documenting the positive impacts of touch and making it part of training
will make care work more meaningful and rewarding, and may reduce pers

onnel compassion fatigue, improving
their wellbeing and increasing the chances of effective person-centred care.13,26 The specific touch awareness
acquired (or rediscovered) by health professionals is fundamental to understanding and advocating for when
touch is inappropriate, or when it is the right tool to use for the right person and in the right setting.7,27
c. Lead to the co-creation of meaningful communication tools. Touch is a multi-sense phenomenon that
communicates “beyond words”16 (p. 206). Several frameworks that support, improve, and orientate care for
people living with dementia use methodologies and approaches that include touch, sounds, and calming
presence to create a therapeutic environment that promotes engagement, independence, and wellbeing. Touch
can serve as a preparatory element for many interventions/caring activities and provide space-time
reorientation, early mobilization, minimization of restraint use, and adequate sleep hygiene, which are all known
to reduce agitation in dementia.1,15,24

r this project.
Ethical considerations
The Social Care Research Ethics Committee review of the study will be obtained via Integrated Research Application System.
The ethical process and approval will be crucial for this project, as working in such a delicate setting (care homes) will require
several steps at the very beginning of the project. The PPIE group will help shape research tools and adapt them for the specific
care setting.
Researchers will ensure that residents, staff, and relatives are informed of and understand ethnographic observation and data
collection, both verbally and by using visual aids like small posters and leaflets. The study will be inclusive of people who lack
capacity or lose capacity to provide consent, in keeping with the EDI view that a lack of capacity to consent should not be grounds
for exclusion by the team. Researchers will align with the care home measures to safeguard residents by attending the specific
training.
PPIE
The project has the support of PPIE group members from the Exeter Academy of Nursing. The PPIE chair (Miss Faye Doris) and
group member Mr Howard Almond have reviewed this application and pledged to support this research. Mr Almond

he safe and respectful
involvement of residents.
Equality, Diversity, and Inclusion
As PI I will use my knowledge and experience as departmental EDI lead to ensure that the principles of equality, diversity, and
inclusion are respected throughout the project.
Residents: As the research area is not very ethnically diverse, we will focus on differences in social background and class among
residents to enhance participant diversity, e.g. the ratio between privately/local authority-funded residents. The chosen care
homes include both rural and urban populations, and one is in a deprived area of South Devon.
Care home staff: The care home workforce, predominantly international staff, plays a crucial role in enriching the research
landscape. Their diverse perspectives, cultural backgrounds, and experiences regarding touch in care are invaluable contributions
to the research on cultural and ethnic diversity.
Research material, including posters, leaflets, and information sheets, will be carefully adapted and tested to meet the cultural
and cognitive needs of the residents and their relatives.

Ethical considerations
The Social Care Research Ethics Committee review of the study will be obtained via Integrated Research Application System.
The ethical process and approval will be crucial for this project, as working in such a delicate setting (care homes) will require
several steps at the very beginning of the project. The PPIE group will help shape research tools and adapt them for the specific
care setting.
Researchers will ensure that residents, staff, and relatives are informed of and understand ethnographic observation and data
collection, both verbally and by using visual aids like small posters and leaflets. The study will be inclusive of people who lack
capacity or lose capacity to provide consent, in keeping with the EDI view that a lack of capacity to consent should not be grounds
for exclusion by the team. Researchers will align with the care home measures to safeguard residents by attending the specific
training.
PPIE

evidence.text:
rn what staff know (and don’t know) about the different types of
touch and how they affect residents.
Methods
1.Scoping review
It consists in a literature review to map out What do (and don’t) we know about how touch is used in the care of older people,
dementia care, care homes and social care settings.
2.Ethnographic study in three care homes
An ethnographic approach means carefully observing people in their real-world activities, and using conversations and interviews
to gain in-depth insight into how people interact in care homes.
3.Design training on “Awareness of interpersonal physical contact during caring encounters.”
1&2 will give us the information to co-design training alongside care workers, customising it for care-homes and truly improving
care through more meaningful touch.
Patient and public involvement (PPIE)
Exeter Academy of Nursing PPIE chair Faye Doris and member Howard Almond (himself a dementia carer) support this research.
Our PPIE group of residents, relatives, and staff will ensure not only that study design and materials are appropriate, but also that
all ethical and safeguarding issues are covered, including consent. PPIE members will feed back during the

evidence.text:
for people with dementia and cognitive impairment.33 The
ethnographic data analysis is an iterative process that starts during fieldwork and includes reflection, which is fundamental to
value the different interpretations emerging during the analysis itself.
Stage 3. Design of a training on “Awareness of Interpersonal physical contact during caring encounters.” Training design will be
guided by the scoping review and ethnographic study. Ethnographic methods ensure person-centred design and customized
interventions for effective implementation in specific contexts. An experience-based co-design approach will effectively and
meaningfully engage care-home staff in the organic development of the training, ensuring it is tailored to their specific
needs/setting and guided by their expert lived experience. Patient and Public Involvement and Engagement (PPIE) members and
stakeholders will provide feedback during the training development, and the final version will be presented during a half-day
seminar to stakeholders and staff. This will help to develop future grant applications for a feasibility study to roll out training on a
larger scale.

project to an NIHR Peninsula Public Engagement Group virtual ideas café, gaining valuable feedback.
Residents, their family members/significant others, and care-home workers (one representative from each home) will be invited to
join the project PPIE group. They will advise and guide study design, recruitment, and dissemination of materials and activities.
They will also review participant information leaflets and consent forms and suggest ways to disseminate findings.
The bespoke PPIE group will meet for one hour at key stages of the project, such as protocol review, recruitment, data collection,
and dissemination, up to eight times. Members will be remunerated in accordance with NIHR guidance.

include local, national, and international impact in care homes and HEIs.
Advisory board
The Advisory Board will meet four times throughout the 2-year project. Members include NHS England (Jane Bunce), NHS Devon
Integrated Care Board (Stevie Morris), Chief Nurse for Social Care (Debra Sturdy), Skills for Care Project Manager (Lucy
Gillespie), Devon Training Hub-Primary Care (TBC), Dementia UK (TBC). These members, including health economist Prof
Hulme and neuroscientist Prof Cazzato (Liverpool University) will actively contribute to the study's development at all stages, from
design to implementation to knowledge exchange. The PPIE lead will facilitate communication between the PPIE and advisory
groups.

evidence.text:
n a blueprint for a touch-focused package of training for care-home workers.
Objectives:
Objective 1: To observe, adopting an ethnographic approach, touch interactions between residents and staff that occur during
caring and daily activities in a care home.
Objective 2: To analyse the interpretations of physical contact and touch interactions in care activities that emerge from interviews
with 3 care homes’ residents, staff, and relatives.
Objective 3: To co-create an educational intervention focused on touch, tailored to care-home staff and residents’ needs by co-
participation design and practice development.
5. Project plan
The RfPB grant will support a research project involving care home residents, staff, and relatives. (The term ‘care home’ includes
nursing and residential homes.)
The project will proceed in three phases:
Stage 1. Scoping review - Map available evidence on the use of interpersonal physical contact (including the diverse types of
touch techniques) in clinical settings, and report on the types of evidence that address and inform practice in the field and the way
the research has been conducted. Using the Joanna Briggs Institute framework for scoping reviews enh
